content:
To find the largest value of $n$ such that the sequence of circles can be constructed, let's consider the pattern in the radii of the circles and the number of circles tangent to line A on each side of point P.

The radius of circle $C_n$ is $1 + 2(2008) = 4017$ as we consider adding 2008 each time as each $C_n$ will be tangent both externally to $B$ and internally to $C_{n-1}$ so the new radius on side of B will contribute a 2008 value.

For circle $C_n$ to be constructed through it where all  n circles are tangent to line A on the right of P, the distance from the center of circle $B$ to the center of circle $C_n$ must not include a point of tangency with $C_1$ with radius $r$ such as to allow $r$ or greater distance to line A.

However in the process of adding circle $C_1, \dots , C_n$, the center point of $C_{n}$ will be $2(2008) + 4017 - (n - 1)$ units to the right of $B$'s center since with each $C_i$ we increase the radius $1$ for that circle. This gives total additional distance $4017 + (n - 1)$. 

Now $4017$ is less than $5000$ so the circle $C_n$ cannot add any extra distance from the previous $C_n$ that would be over half the radius of a new circle with a radius of $r$ with $r > 1$.

So with radius $r$ and with each circle we can keep adding radius to $C_{n-1}$. Thus we can construct circles through to point where $4017 + 2008 - r = 6015 - r$. When $6015 - r > 1$, then a circle of radius $r$ can also be constructed.

Now let's calculate $n > 1$ for which we have  $4017 + 2007(n - 1) > 1$. 

$4017 + 2008(n - 1) > 1$. We can add $1$ to each side, then subtract $4017$, then divide each side by $2008$. This gives $\frac{4017 + 1}{2008} + (n - 1) > \frac{1}{2008}$.

$(n - 1) > \frac{4018}{2008}  - \frac{1}{2008}$ which gives $n >\left( \frac{4018}{2008}  -  \frac{1 - 4018}{2008} 
\right) + 1$. Solving for $n$ yields $n > ( 2) + 1 = 3$.

The value of n = {3}
